scene.mamba.title: Mamba — selective copying
mamba.ch.intro.title: The task
mamba.ch.intro.body: Selective copying is a memory test. The first 16 positions hold 4 data tokens (ids 1–8) scattered among NOISE tokens (id 9); then a GO marker (id 10); then the model must reproduce the 4 data tokens, in their original order. To win it has to notice and store each data token as it streams past, skip the noise, and hold what it stored until GO. Every cell on screen is a real number from a 2-layer Mamba (d_model 48) that solves this at over 99% accuracy — nothing here is mocked. The same 21-token input runs through every architecture in this atlas, so you can compare how each machine solves the identical problem.
mamba.ch.embed.title: Embedding
mamba.ch.embed.body: Each of the 21 token ids looks up one row of the embedding table E (16 ids × 48 features) and copies it into the residual stream — the tall 21 × 48 grid down the middle that every later stage reads from and writes back to. From here on the tokens are gone; the model works entirely with these continuous 48-dimensional vectors. Hover any cell to see its exact value and the row and column it came from. The glowing thread down the centre is that residual stream — the spine the whole computation hangs on.
mamba.ch.block.title: The block
mamba.ch.block.body: A Mamba block reads the residual stream, computes a correction, and adds it back — it never overwrites, only edits. The sequence is: RMSNorm rescales each position; in_proj expands 48 → 192 and splits it into a value path x and a gate z; a depthwise causal conv mixes each channel over the last few positions; the selective SSM scans along time; the gate z filters the result; and out_proj projects 96 → 48 to write the correction back. The matrices beside each stage are the learned weights it multiplies by — follow the spine to see where each result rejoins the stream.
mamba.ch.conv.title: Local mixing
mamba.ch.conv.body: Before the long-range scan, a depthwise 1-D convolution (kernel width 4) lets every channel blend the current position with the previous three. Depthwise means each of the 96 channels has its own small filter — no mixing across channels here. Causal means it only looks backward, never at future tokens, so the model stays a valid left-to-right predictor. This is cheap, local context; remembering a data token from 15 steps ago is the scan's job, not the conv's.
mamba.ch.selection.title: Selection
mamba.ch.selection.body: This stage is the whole point of Mamba, and where it departs from older state-space models. The step size Δ and the scan inputs B and C are not fixed weights — they are computed from the current token, by x_proj and dt_proj. A linear time-invariant SSM reuses one A, B, C at every position, so it treats every token the same. Here Δ, B, C change token by token, which lets the model decide, per token, how much to write into memory and how much to read out. That input-dependence is what 'selective' means — and it is exactly what a copy-and-ignore task needs.
mamba.ch.scan.title: The scan
mamba.ch.scan.body: This is the recurrence that carries memory through time, run one token at a time. The state h is a fixed-size grid (d_inner 96 × d_state 8) — it never grows, however long the sequence. At each step h ← exp(Δ⊙A)·h + Δ⊙B·x: the first term decays the old state, the second writes in the new token, both scaled by the input-dependent Δ. When Δ ≈ 0 the new token is essentially ignored and the old memory passes through intact; when Δ is large the token is written in strongly. Play the scan and watch Δ (layer0.delta.out) stay small on NOISE positions and open up on data tokens — that selective gate is the copying, happening in front of you.
mamba.ch.gate.title: Gate & output
mamba.ch.gate.body: The scan produces an output y, one 96-vector per position. Before it can rejoin the stream it passes a gate: y is multiplied elementwise by SiLU(z), where z is the second half of in_proj from back at the block's start. The gate decides, per channel, how much of the scan's output actually survives. out_proj then projects the gated 96-vector back down to 48 and adds it onto the residual stream — the correction this block contributes.
mamba.ch.layer2.title: Second block
mamba.ch.layer2.body: The residual stream, now carrying the first block's edits, flows into a second identical block — same machine, same shapes, different learned weights. Stacking lets the model build on what the first block found: the first pass might mark which positions are data, the second refine what to emit. Two layers is enough for this toy task; production Mamba models stack dozens.
mamba.ch.readout.title: Readout
mamba.ch.readout.body: After the last block a final RMSNorm rescales the stream, then lm_head projects each 48-vector to 16 logits — one score per vocabulary id. The model's prediction at each position is the argmax of its logits. Look at the four answer positions, just after GO: their argmax picks out the four data tokens, in order. Read them off head.logits and compare to the input — that is the copy, done.
mamba.ch.recap.title: Recap
mamba.ch.recap.body: One fixed-size state, updated through an input-dependent gate, copied four tokens across a noisy sequence — with no attention matrix and no growing cache. The work per token stays constant as the sequence grows, which is what makes state-space models attractive for very long inputs. Now open the GPT page: the same input, the same task, but solved by attention recomputing a full token-by-token comparison instead of carrying a running state.
scene.gpt.title: GPT — selective copying
gpt.ch.intro.title: The task
gpt.ch.intro.body: The same selective-copying input — 4 data tokens hidden in noise, a GO marker, then reproduce them in order — now solved by a 2-layer Transformer (d_model 48, 3 attention heads). Where Mamba carried a running state forward, GPT does something different: at every position it looks back over the whole sequence at once and decides what to pull in. Every cell is a real value from a model that solves the task at over 99% accuracy. Step through to watch attention reach back to the data tokens.
gpt.ch.embed.title: Tokens + positions
gpt.ch.embed.body: GPT adds two embeddings per position: one for the token id (which row of the token table) and one for the position index (which row of the position table). Attention has no built-in sense of order — to it a sequence is a set — so position must be injected explicitly here. This is a real difference from Mamba, whose recurrence is ordered by construction and needs no position embedding. Their sum is the residual stream the blocks read and write.
gpt.ch.attention.title: Attention
gpt.ch.attention.body: Each head projects the stream into queries, keys, and values. It scores every pair of positions as Q·Kᵀ/√d_head, then masks the upper triangle to −∞ so a position can never attend to the future — that is the dark triangle in the scores. A softmax over each row turns the scores into weights that sum to 1: a soft selection of which earlier positions to read. The answer positions learn to put their weight on the data tokens — read the bright cells in the weight matrix to see it. Crucially this T×T map is recomputed from scratch every forward pass; nothing is carried between steps, the opposite of Mamba's running state.
gpt.ch.mlp.title: MLP
gpt.ch.mlp.body: Attention moved information between positions; the MLP now transforms each position on its own. It expands 48 → 192, applies a GELU nonlinearity, and projects 192 → 48 back into the stream. Every position goes through the same MLP independently — no mixing across positions here. The wide 192-dim hidden layer is where most of the block's parameters live, and where per-token features are computed.
gpt.ch.readout.title: Readout
gpt.ch.readout.body: A final LayerNorm, then lm_head projects each position's 48-vector to 16 logits. At the four answer positions the argmax recovers the copied data tokens — the same result Mamba reached, but by a different route: attention re-reading the data tokens on demand, rather than a fixed-size state carrying them forward. Same task, two machines — that contrast is what this atlas is built around.
scene.rwkv.title: RWKV — selective copying
rwkv.ch.intro.title: The task
rwkv.ch.intro.body: The same selective-copying input, now solved by RWKV — a model that trains like attention but runs like an RNN. Like Mamba it carries a fixed-size state forward (constant cost per token), but through a different mechanism: a per-channel weighted average of past values with exponential decay, plus a gated squared-ReLU MLP. Every cell is a real value from a 2-layer RWKV (d_model 48) that solves the task at over 99% accuracy. Watch how the decaying state accumulates the data tokens.
rwkv.ch.embed.title: Embedding
rwkv.ch.embed.body: Each token id looks up a row of the embedding table (16 × 48) into the residual stream. RWKV has no position embedding — like Mamba, order is built into its recurrence rather than supplied separately. Every block reads this stream and adds a correction back to it.
rwkv.ch.timemix.title: Time-mixing (WKV)
rwkv.ch.timemix.body: RWKV's linear attention. A token-shift first blends each position with the previous one (a learned per-channel mix), then r, k, v are linear maps of the blended input. The WKV output at position t is a weighted average of all past values v: each earlier value decays by exp(−(t−i)·w) with a per-channel decay w, and the current token gets a bonus u. This is attention without a T×T matrix — a running numerator/denominator state, so the cost stays constant per token. Then r = σ(·) gates it: out = (r ⊙ wkv)·Wo. Look at the decay vector (att.0.time_decay): slow-decay channels carry information far down the sequence, fast-decay channels stay local.
rwkv.ch.channelmix.title: Channel-mixing
rwkv.ch.channelmix.body: After time-mixing moves information across positions, the channel-mix transforms each position on its own. Token-shift again, then k = relu(x̂·Wk)² — a squared-ReLU over a wide 192-dim hidden layer — and the result is gated: σ(r) · (k·Wv). This is RWKV's MLP, the counterpart of GPT's feed-forward block, here with a squared-ReLU and a receptance gate.
rwkv.ch.readout.title: Readout
rwkv.ch.readout.body: A final LayerNorm, then the head projects each position to 16 logits. At the answer positions the argmax recovers the copied tokens — the same result as Mamba and GPT, reached this time by RWKV's decaying-state attention. Three machines, one task: a fixed-size selective state (Mamba), a full attention matrix (GPT), and a decaying weighted sum (RWKV).
scene.moe.title: MoE — selective copying
moe.ch.intro.title: The task
moe.ch.intro.body: The same selective-copying input, now solved by a Mixture-of-Experts Transformer. Attention is unchanged from GPT; what changes is the MLP — instead of one dense feed-forward block, each token is routed to a few specialized experts. A router scores the experts per token, the top 2 of 4 are chosen, and only those contribute, so the model holds more parameters than it spends on any single token. Every cell is a real value from a 2-layer MoE that solves the task at over 99% accuracy.
moe.ch.embed.title: Tokens + positions
moe.ch.embed.body: Identical to GPT: each position adds a token embedding and a position embedding into the residual stream. The blocks that follow keep GPT's attention and swap only the MLP for a routed mixture.
moe.ch.attention.title: Attention
moe.ch.attention.body: Unchanged from GPT: each head scores Q·Kᵀ/√d, masks the future to −∞, softmaxes each row, and mixes the values. The answer positions attend back to the data tokens. MoE leaves attention dense — only the feed-forward stage becomes sparse.
moe.ch.moe.title: Mixture of experts
moe.ch.moe.body: The routed feed-forward. A router (a small linear map) scores the 4 experts for each token and softmaxes them — read moe.router. The top 2 are kept and renormalized to sum to 1 — moe.gates, the routing decision. Each expert is its own MLP (fc → GELU → proj); all four are shown running on every token, but a token's actual output (moe.out) is the gate-weighted sum of only its top-2 experts. That is the point of MoE: more experts add capacity without adding the per-token compute.
moe.ch.readout.title: Readout
moe.ch.readout.body: A final LayerNorm, then lm_head to 16 logits; the argmax at the answer positions recovers the copied tokens. The same task once more, solved this time by sparse routing. Four machines now share this atlas: Mamba's selective state, GPT's attention, RWKV's decaying sum, and MoE's routed experts.
scene.kan.title: KAN — selective copying
kan.ch.intro.title: The task
kan.ch.intro.body: The same selective-copying input, now solved by a KAN — a Kolmogorov–Arnold network. A standard MLP puts fixed activations on the nodes and learns weights on the edges; a KAN flips that and learns a univariate function on every edge instead. Classic KANs use B-splines for those functions; this one uses the FastKAN form — each edge function is a learned sum of fixed Gaussian bumps. Attention is GPT's; only the feed-forward is a KAN. Every cell is a real value from a 2-layer model that solves the task at 100% accuracy.
kan.ch.embed.title: Tokens + positions
kan.ch.embed.body: Identical to GPT: each position adds a token embedding and a position embedding into the residual stream. The blocks keep GPT's attention and replace only the feed-forward with a KAN.
kan.ch.attention.title: Attention
kan.ch.attention.body: Unchanged from GPT: each head scores Q·Kᵀ/√d, masks the future, softmaxes, and mixes the values; the answer positions attend back to the data tokens. The KAN, like MoE, leaves attention dense and changes only the feed-forward.
kan.ch.kan.title: Learnable edges
kan.ch.kan.body: The KAN feed-forward. Instead of fc → GELU → proj, each input is run through learnable edge functions. Concretely: every scalar input is evaluated against 8 fixed Gaussian bumps on a grid — that is kan.rbf, the 48 × 8 = 384 basis activations (the learned edge functions, sampled at the input). A linear map mixes those into the output (spline), a residual base branch base = silu(x̂)·W_base runs alongside, and the two sum to kan.out. The expressiveness lives in the per-edge spline coefficients, not in a wide hidden layer.
kan.ch.readout.title: Readout
kan.ch.readout.body: A final LayerNorm, then lm_head to 16 logits; the argmax recovers the copied tokens. Five machines now share this atlas — Mamba's selective state, GPT's attention, RWKV's decaying sum, MoE's routed experts, and KAN's learnable edges — every one of them solving the same selective copy.

ui.landing.title: Beyond Transformers
ui.landing.subtitle: A 3D, tensor-level tour of LLM architectures — real toy weights, computed live in your browser. The same selective-copying task, many different machines.
card.mamba.title: Mamba
card.mamba.desc: Selective state space — a fixed-size recurrent state with an input-dependent gate.
